questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a neutral carbon atom in its ground state, the mass is
options: dependent on the form of carbon, such as graphite or charcoal
questions.solution:
The unified atomic mass unit ($1 \text{ u}$) is defined as exactly $\dfrac{1}{12}$ of the mass of an unbound, neutral carbon-12 atom in its nuclear and electronic ground state.

Therefore, the mass of a standard neutral carbon atom (carbon-12) in its ground state is exactly $12 \text{ u}$.

The mass of an individual atom is an intrinsic property and does not depend on the macroscopic allotropic form (such as graphite, diamond, or charcoal) it may be a part of.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The mass number of a nucleus is equivalent to
options: the number of protons inside the nucleus
the number of nucleons inside the nucleus
the mass of the nucleus in atomic mass units
the number of neutrons inside the nucleus
questions.solution:
The mass number of a nucleus is defined as the sum of the number of protons and the number of neutrons present in it.

Protons and neutrons are collectively referred to as nucleons.

Thus, the mass number is equal to the total number of nucleons inside the nucleus.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In comparison to the $^{12}\text{C}$ atom, the $^{14}\text{C}$ atom possesses
options: two extra neutrons and two extra electrons
two extra neutrons and no extra electron
two extra protons but no extra electron
two extra protons and two extra electrons
questions.solution:
Carbon has an atomic number $Z = 6$.

For the $^{12}\text{C}$ atom, the number of protons is $6$, the number of electrons is $6$, and the number of neutrons is $12 - 6 = 6$.

For the $^{14}\text{C}$ atom, the number of protons is $6$, the number of electrons is $6$, and the number of neutrons is $14 - 6 = 8$.

Therefore, the $^{14}\text{C}$ atom possesses two extra neutrons and no extra electron compared to the $^{12}\text{C}$ atom.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The mass number of an atomic nucleus is
options: always equal to its atomic number
always larger than its atomic number
always smaller than its atomic number
sometimes larger than and sometimes equal to its atomic number
questions.solution:
The mass number $A$ of an atomic nucleus is the sum of the number of protons $Z$ (atomic number) and the number of neutrons $N$. This gives the relation $A = Z + N$.

For an ordinary hydrogen nucleus ($^{1}H$), there are no neutrons ($N = 0$), so its mass number is equal to its atomic number ($A = Z = 1$).

For all other atomic nuclei, there is at least one neutron ($N > 0$), making the mass number strictly greater than the atomic number ($A > Z$).

Therefore, the mass number is sometimes larger than and sometimes equal to its atomic number.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suppose $F_{pp}$, $F_{pn}$, and $F_{nn}$ represent the magnitudes of the nuclear force exerted by a proton on a proton, a proton on a neutron, and a neutron on a neutron, respectively. For a separation distance of $1 \text{ fm}$,
questions.solution:
The strong nuclear force is a fundamental force that acts between nucleons (protons and neutrons).

One of the key properties of the strong nuclear force is that it is charge-independent. This means that the nuclear force between any two nucleons does not depend on their electric charges.

Therefore, for a given separation distance (such as $1 \text{ fm}$), the magnitude of the nuclear force between two protons ($F_{pp}$), a proton and a neutron ($F_{pn}$), and two neutrons ($F_{nn}$) is exactly the same.

$F_{pp} = F_{pn} = F_{nn}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a nucleus having radius $R$ and mass number $A$, the plot of $\ln(R/R_0)$ against $\ln A$ is
questions.solution:
The radius of a nucleus $R$ is related to its mass number $A$ by the formula:

$R = R_0 A^{1/3}$

Rearranging the terms, we get:

$\dfrac{R}{R_0} = A^{1/3}$

Taking the natural logarithm on both sides:

$\ln\left(\dfrac{R}{R_0}\right) = \ln(A^{1/3})$

$\ln\left(\dfrac{R}{R_0}\right) = \dfrac{1}{3} \ln A$

Let $y = \ln\left(\dfrac{R}{R_0}\right)$ and $x = \ln A$. The equation becomes:

$y = \dfrac{1}{3} x$

This represents the equation of a straight line passing through the origin with a slope of $\dfrac{1}{3}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider $F_{pp}$, $F_{pn}$, and $F_{nn}$ as the magnitudes of the net force exerted by a proton on a proton, by a proton on a neutron, and by a neutron on a neutron, respectively. Disregarding gravitational force, at a separation of $1 \text{ fm}$,
questions.solution:
At a separation of $1 \text{ fm}$, the strong nuclear force between nucleons is attractive.

The strong nuclear force is charge-independent, meaning the magnitude of the nuclear force between any pair of nucleons is the same. Let this force be $F_N$.

For a proton-neutron pair and a neutron-neutron pair, the electrostatic force is zero. Thus, the net force is entirely due to the strong nuclear force:

$F_{pn} = F_{nn} = F_N$

For a proton-proton pair, in addition to the attractive strong nuclear force, there is a repulsive electrostatic force $F_E$ between the two positively charged protons.

Since the nuclear force is much stronger than the electrostatic force at this distance, the net force is attractive but its magnitude is reduced by the electrostatic repulsion:

$F_{pp} = F_N - F_E$

Therefore, we can conclude that $F_{pp} < F_{pn} = F_{nn}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two protons are positioned at a separation of $10 \text{ nm}$. Let $F_n$ and $F_e$ denote the nuclear force and the electromagnetic force between them, respectively. Which of the following is true?
questions.solution:
The nuclear force is a short-range force, effective only up to distances of the order of a few femtometers ($1 \text{ fm} = 10^{-15} \text{ m}$).

The given separation between the protons is $10 \text{ nm} = 10 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 10^{-8} \text{ m}$.

At this distance, the nuclear force $F_n$ is negligibly small and practically zero.

The electromagnetic force $F_e$ is a long-range force and varies as $\dfrac{1}{r^2}$. At $10 \text{ nm}$, it has a finite, non-zero value.

Therefore, at a separation of $10 \text{ nm}$, $F_e \gg F_n$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: With an increase in the mass number $A$, the binding energy per nucleon of a nucleus
options: varies in a manner dependent on the specific value of $A$
questions.solution:
The binding energy per nucleon curve shows that for low mass numbers, the binding energy per nucleon generally increases with an increase in mass number $A$.

It reaches a maximum value of about $8.8 \text{ MeV}$ for $A \approx 56$ (Iron).

For mass numbers greater than $56$, the binding energy per nucleon gradually decreases, reaching about $7.6 \text{ MeV}$ for Uranium.

Therefore, the binding energy per nucleon does not monotonically increase or decrease, but varies in a manner dependent on the specific value of the mass number $A$.

questions.subject: Physics
options: more than half of the active nuclei decay
half of the active nuclei decay
fewer than half of the active nuclei decay
questions.solution:
The number of active nuclei remaining after time $t$ is given by the radioactive decay law:

$N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$

The average life (or mean life) of a radioactive substance is $\tau = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$.

Substituting $t = \tau = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ in the decay equation:

$N = N_0 e^{-\lambda \left(\dfrac{1}{\lambda}\right)} = N_0 e^{-1} = \dfrac{N_0}{e}$

Since $e \approx 2.718$, the number of remaining active nuclei is:

$N = \dfrac{N_0}{2.718} \approx 0.368 N_0$

The number of nuclei that have decayed during this time is:

$N_{decayed} = N_0 - N = N_0 - 0.368 N_0 = 0.632 N_0$

This means approximately $63.2\%$ of the initial active nuclei decay during one average-life, which is more than half.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following statements provides an incorrect description of the binding energy of a nucleus?
options: It is the total kinetic energy of all the nucleons contained within the nucleus.
It is equal to the total rest mass energy of the individual nucleons minus the rest mass energy of the assembled nucleus.
It represents the energy needed to separate a nucleus into its individual constituent nucleons.
It is the energy released when free nucleons fuse together to form a nucleus.
questions.solution:
The binding energy of a nucleus is defined as the minimum energy required to completely separate the nucleus into its constituent nucleons. Equivalently, it is the energy released when free nucleons come together to form the nucleus.

Mathematically, it is equal to the mass defect multiplied by the square of the speed of light, which is the difference between the total rest mass energy of the individual nucleons and the rest mass energy of the assembled nucleus.

Binding energy is not the total kinetic energy of the nucleons contained within the nucleus. Therefore, the statement regarding kinetic energy is incorrect.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: During a radioactive decay process, both the atomic number and the mass number remain unchanged. Identify the particle that is emitted in this decay.
questions.solution:
In a radioactive decay process, if both the atomic number and the mass number of the nucleus remain unchanged, the decay corresponds to gamma emission.

During gamma decay, a nucleus in an excited state transitions to a lower energy state by emitting a high-energy electromagnetic radiation, which is a photon.

For the other options:
Proton emission decreases both the atomic number and the mass number by $1$.
Neutron emission decreases the mass number by $1$ while the atomic number remains unchanged.
Electron emission (beta decay) increases the atomic number by $1$ while the mass number remains unchanged.

Thus, the emitted particle is a photon.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the process of negative beta decay,
options: a proton within the nucleus decays and emits an electron
an electron already residing inside the nucleus is expelled
a neutron within the nucleus decays and emits an electron
questions.solution:
In negative beta decay ($\beta^{-}$ decay), a neutron inside the nucleus is unstable and transforms into a proton, an electron, and an electron antineutrino.

The nuclear reaction is given by:
$n \rightarrow p + e^{-} + \bar{\nu}_{e}$

The emitted electron ($e^{-}$) is expelled from the nucleus as a beta particle. Electrons do not pre-exist inside the nucleus; they are created during the decay process.

Therefore, a neutron within the nucleus decays and emits an electron.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A newly prepared radioactive source with a half-life of $2 \text{ h}$ emits radiation at an intensity $64$ times the allowable safe level. The minimum time required before one can safely work with this source is
questions.solution:
Let the safe level of radiation intensity be $I_0$.

The initial intensity of the radioactive source is $I = 64 I_0$.

The intensity after $n$ half-lives is given by $I(t) = I \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^n$.

For the source to be safe to work with, we require $I(t) \le I_0$.

$64 I_0 \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^n \le I_0$

$\dfrac{64}{2^n} \le 1$

$2^n \ge 64$

Since $64 = 2^6$, we get $n = 6$.

The minimum number of half-lives required is $6$.

Given the half-life $T_{1/2} = 2 \text{ h}$, the minimum time required is $t = n \times T_{1/2} = 6 \times 2 \text{ h} = 12 \text{ h}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A radioactive sample has a decay constant $\lambda$. The half-life and average-life of the sample are respectively
questions.solution:
The radioactive decay law is given by $N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$

For half-life $T_{1/2}$, the number of active nuclei becomes half of its initial value, so $N = \dfrac{N_0}{2}$

$\dfrac{N_0}{2} = N_0 e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$

$e^{\lambda T_{1/2}} = 2$

$T_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda}$

The average life or mean life $T_{avg}$ is defined as the sum of the lifetimes of all individual nuclei divided by the total number of nuclei, which is the reciprocal of the decay constant.

$T_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$

Thus, the half-life and average-life are $\dfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda}$ and $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ respectively.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Free $^{238}\text{U}$ nuclei placed inside a train emit alpha particles. When the train is stationary and a uranium nucleus decays, a passenger measures the separation between the alpha particle and the recoiling nucleus to be $x$ at a time $t$ after the decay. If a decay occurs while the train is moving at a uniform speed $v$, the distance between the alpha particle and the recoiling nucleus at a time $t$ after the decay, as measured by the passenger, will be
options: dependent on the direction of the train
questions.solution:
The train moving at a uniform speed $v$ represents an inertial frame of reference.

According to the principle of Galilean relativity, the laws of physics and the internal dynamics of a system remain the same in all inertial frames.

Since the $^{238}\text{U}$ nucleus is placed inside the train, it is initially at rest with respect to the passenger in both cases. The velocities of the emitted alpha particle and the recoiling nucleus relative to the passenger will be identical to those in the stationary case.

Alternatively, if $\vec{v}_{\alpha}$ and $\vec{v}_{R}$ are the velocities of the alpha particle and the recoiling nucleus in the train's frame, the separation at time $t$ is $|\vec{v}_{\alpha} - \vec{v}_{R}|t = x$.

Therefore, the separation between the alpha particle and the recoiling nucleus at time $t$, as measured by the passenger, remains $x$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Ten grams of $^{57}\text{Co}$ is placed in an open container and undergoes beta-decay with a half-life of $270 \text{ days}$. After $540 \text{ days}$, the weight of the material inside the container will be approximately
questions.solution:
In $\beta$-decay, the mass number of the nucleus remains unchanged. The parent nucleus $^{57}\text{Co}$ decays into a daughter nucleus which is also a solid and remains in the open container.

The mass of the emitted $\beta$-particles and the mass equivalent of the energy released are negligible compared to the total mass of the material. Thus, the atomic mass of the daughter nucleus is approximately equal to that of the parent nucleus.

After $540 \text{ days}$ (which corresponds to exactly two half-lives, since $T_{1/2} = 270 \text{ days}$), the mass of $^{57}\text{Co}$ remaining is $10 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 = 2.5 \text{ g}$.

The mass of the daughter nucleus formed is $10 - 2.5 = 7.5 \text{ g}$.

Since both the parent and daughter nuclei are solid and present in the container, the total weight of the material inside the container remains approximately $2.5 + 7.5 = 10 \text{ g}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a $^{14}\text{N}$ nucleus is bombarded with an $\alpha$-particle, a $^{17}\text{O}$ nucleus is produced along with the emission of another particle. The emitted particle is a
questions.solution:
The nuclear reaction can be written as:

$^{14}_{7}\text{N} + ^{4}_{2}\text{He} \rightarrow ^{17}_{8}\text{O} + ^{A}_{Z}\text{X}$

Applying the law of conservation of mass number:

$14 + 4 = 17 + A$

$A = 1$

Applying the law of conservation of atomic number:

$7 + 2 = 8 + Z$

$Z = 1$

The particle with mass number $1$ and atomic number $1$ is a proton ($^{1}_{1}\text{p}$).

questions.subject: Physics
options: two light nuclei merge to form a heavier nucleus and possibly other products
a heavy nucleus divides into two fragments by itself
a light nucleus breaks apart when bombarded by thermal neutrons
a heavy nucleus breaks apart when bombarded by thermal neutrons
questions.solution:
Nuclear fission is a process in which a heavy nucleus (such as $^{235}\text{U}$) splits into two lighter nuclei of comparable masses when bombarded by a low-energy thermal neutron.

The merging of two light nuclei is called nuclear fusion.

Therefore, in a nuclear fission reaction, a heavy nucleus breaks apart when bombarded by thermal neutrons.
